original_hypothesis.title: CustomPoolDeployer.createCustomPool Lacks Access Control Allowing Arbitrary Pool Creation with Manipulated Prices
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: createCustomPool has no access control modifier (unlike setPlugin/setFee which use onlyAuthorized). Anyone can call it to create Algebra CL pools with arbitrary initialPrice values. Created pools are registered in algebraPoolAPIStorage as legitimate, potentially misleading routing algorithms and price oracles that query registered pools.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Other privileged functions in the contract use onlyAuthorized, but createCustomPool does not. The function registers the pool in algebraPoolAPIStorage.setDeployerForPair(), making it appear as a valid protocol pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No onlyOwner or onlyAuthorized modifier on createCustomPool; other functions use onlyAuthorized

preconditions: entryPoint allows the deployer to create pools (likely by design)
Attacker can call createCustomPool with arbitrary parameters

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: initialPrice is attacker-controlled, sets arbitrary starting price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create pool with extreme price, registered as legitimate in algebraPoolAPIStorage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Mislead routing to swap through manipulated-price pool

economic_questions: Does the entryPoint restrict pool creation beyond the deployer hook?
Do routing algorithms (BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut) use registered pools for quoting?
Can attacker add minimal liquidity to manipulated pool to execute exploitative swaps?
validation_requirements: Call createCustomPool as non-authorized account and verify success
Check if BlackholePairAPIV2 routes through attacker-created pools

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for pool creation (~0.1 AVAX) + minimal liquidity

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal seed liquidity at manipulated price
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could poison routing if pools are used for price discovery

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check entryPoint access control for deployer-initiated creation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test routing algorithm inclusion of attacker pools

validation_result.mechanism: missing access control on pool creation
validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can call createCustomPool to create Algebra CL pools with arbitrary initial prices, which get registered as legitimate protocol pools in algebraPoolAPIStorage, potentially poisoning routing algorithms.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that CustomPoolDeployer.createCustomPool (lines 52-93) lacks any access control modifier, while other state-changing functions in the same contract use onlyAuthorized or onlyOwner. The function creates a real Algebra CL pool, initializes it with a caller-controlled initialPrice, and critically registers it via algebraPoolAPIStorage.setDeployerForPair(customPool). This registration makes the pool appear as a legitimate protocol pool to the routing system. BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut iterates over all concentrated pairs and uses algebraPoolAPIStorage.pairToDeployer() to filter valid pools - pools created through this unguarded function pass this filter. Similarly, RouterV2.getAmountsOut uses the deployer from algebraPoolAPIStorage for quoting. While PairFactory.createPair is also permissionless, it does not allow setting an arbitrary initial price - the price is determined by the first liquidity deposit. In contrast, createCustomPool allows direct price initialization via IAlgebraPool(customPool).initialize(initialPrice), which is a one-time call that permanently sets the starting tick.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: CustomPoolDeployer.sol lines 52-93 show createCustomPool with no access modifier. Lines 96-103 show beforeCreatePoolHook with entryPoint check (separate function). Lines 105-125 show setPluginForPool, setPlugin, setPluginConfig, setFee, setCommunityFee all using onlyAuthorized. The inconsistency is clear. The key registration happens at line 68: IAlgebraPoolAPIStorage(algebraPoolAPIStorage).setDeployerForPair(customPool), which makes the pool visible to routing in BlackholePairAPIV2.sol.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No access control modifier (onlyOwner, onlyAuthorized) on this function. Any external caller can create pools with arbitrary initialPrice values. The pool is registered in algebraPoolAPIStorage, making it visible to the protocol's routing system. Other management functions in the same contract use onlyAuthorized, indicating this is an oversight.

The routing algorithm treats any pool with a non-zero deployer in algebraPoolAPIStorage as legitimate. Pools created by unauthorized callers through createCustomPool get registered here and will be considered for routing.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker calls createCustomPool(attacker, tokenA, tokenB, '0x', manipulatedPrice)
2. entryPoint.createCustomPool creates pool, calls back to beforeCreatePoolHook which returns plugin
5. BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut later iterates concentrated pools, finds attacker's pool via pairToDeployer check
6. If attacker adds minimal liquidity, routing may direct swaps through manipulated pool
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The path starts at createCustomPool with no access control check. The function proceeds to create a pool via the entryPoint (which trusts the CustomPoolDeployer as a registered deployer), initializes it with attacker-controlled price, and registers it in algebraPoolAPIStorage at line 68. This registration is what makes the pool visible to BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut (line 340) and RouterV2.getAmountsOut (line 212), which both use pairToDeployer to validate pools.

deployment_assumptions_checked: createCustomPool is intended to be permissionless
config_validation.deployment_evidence: Deployment script create_custom_pool.js shows the owner account calling createCustomPool, not arbitrary users. The script accesses contracts from a JSON config of specific deployer addresses, suggesting controlled deployment. Additionally, add_custom_deployer_apistorage.js shows custom deployers being explicitly registered, implying pool creation through them should also be controlled.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: 1) Attacker calls createCustomPool with an extreme initialPrice (e.g., representing tokenA being worth 1000000x tokenB). 2) The pool is created and registered in algebraPoolAPIStorage. 3) Attacker adds minimal liquidity at the manipulated price. 4) When BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut is called by the frontend or any router integration, the attacker's pool may be selected as the best route if its quoted output appears favorable. 5) Users who execute swaps through the manipulated pool receive far less value than expected. The attack is feasible because: the function has no access control, the pool gets registered as legitimate, and the routing system includes it in its search space.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is HIGH because: (1) Pool creation is completely uncontrolled, allowing registry pollution with arbitrary pools. (2) Created pools are indistinguishable from admin-created pools in algebraPoolAPIStorage. (3) The routing algorithm in BlackholePairAPIV2 considers these pools for swap routing. (4) Users could be routed through manipulated-price pools. (5) The initialPrice is permanently set and cannot be changed after initialization. Even without profitable exploitation, the registry pollution itself degrades protocol integrity.
recommended_mitigations: Add onlyAuthorized or onlyOwner modifier to createCustomPool function
Alternatively, add a whitelist check for the creator parameter
Consider adding a separate pool creation role that is more restrictive than general authorization
Add validation on initialPrice to ensure it falls within reasonable bounds
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy CustomPoolDeployer with all dependencies. 2. From a non-owner, non-authorized account, call createCustomPool with extreme initialPrice. 3. Verify pool is created and registered in algebraPoolAPIStorage (pairToDeployer returns non-zero). 4. Call BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut and verify the attacker's pool appears in routing results.
uncertainties: The IAlgebraCustomPoolEntryPoint is an external contract whose internal validation logic is unknown - it might have additional restrictions on pool creation
The IAlgebraPoolAPIStorage.setDeployerForPair might have access controls that restrict which callers can register pools (though the CustomPoolDeployer address would likely be whitelisted)
The practical impact depends on whether frontends and users actually use BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut for routing decisions
additional_testing_needed: Test calling createCustomPool from unauthorized account and verify the full flow succeeds
Verify that algebraPoolAPIStorage.setDeployerForPair accepts calls from the CustomPoolDeployer for any pool
Test that BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut actually returns routes through attacker-created pools
Assess whether the entryPoint has additional access controls not visible in this codebase
